title: Strada Education Foundation Opens Applications for the 2026–2027 Student Research Advisory Group
summary: Strada Education Foundation has officially launched the application cycle for the 2026–2027 cohort of the Student Research Advisory Group (SRAG), a paid national initiative designed to elevate student voices in higher education and workforce research. This opportunity invites undergraduate students from across the United States to directly influence research that shapes policy, practice, and the future of postsecondary education. The SRAG program is built on the belief that students are essential partners in designing meaningful, relevant, and rigorous research. By participating, students help ensure that Strada’s research reflects real student experiences, challenges, and aspirations. About the Student Research Advisory Group The Student Research Advisory Group serves as a collaborative body of undergraduate students who work closely with Strada’s Postsecondary Education (PSE) research team. Members act as advisors throughout the research lifecycle, offering insights that strengthen the quality and relevance of Strada’s studies. Key Responsibilities of SRAG Members Advise on active research projects related to: Affordability in higher education Quality coaching and student support Work-based learning and career preparation Provide feedback at multiple stages of the research process, including: Study design Data interpretation Final deliverables Ensure that research outcomes reflect the lived experiences of today’s diverse student population Participate in structured discussions that help shape national conversations on higher education and workforce development Program Structure and Commitment To make participation accessible and manageable for students, SRAG is designed with a flexible and supportive structure: Monthly Virtual Meetings: Members attend one-hour virtual meetings each month, allowing students from any U.S. location to participate without travel barriers. Paid Monthly Stipend: All selected students receive compensation for their time, expertise, and contributions. Collaborative Engagement: Students work directly with Strada’s research team, gaining exposure to professional research environments and contributing to impactful national studies. Application Timeline Applications Open: June 26, 2026 Application Deadline: July 17, 2026 at 11:59 PM PST Applicants must complete the online application form and upload a resume or CV. The application includes demographic questions and short-answer prompts to help Strada understand each applicant’s background, interests, and motivations. Why This Opportunity Matters Strada’s SRAG program is more than a student advisory group—it is a platform for students to influence national research that affects millions of learners. By participating, students gain: A meaningful role in shaping higher education and workforce policy Professional experience in research collaboration Opportunities to amplify student perspectives at a national level A paid, flexible commitment that supports academic schedules This initiative reflects Strada’s commitment to centering student voices in research that drives systemic change. For more information about this opportunity and to apply visit here Discover more fellowship opportunities here The post Strada Education Foundation Opens Applications for the 2026–2027 Student Research Advisory Group appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Coffee & Quality 2026 Fellows Program: A Comprehensive Guide for Nonprofits Strengthening Data & Evaluation Capacity
summary: The Coffee Quality 2026 Fellows Program , hosted by United Way of Greater Houston, is a deeply structured, multi‑month capacity‑building initiative designed to help nonprofit organizations strengthen their data, evaluation, and continuous quality improvement (CQI) practices. This program is intentionally built as a community of practice , offering hands‑on learning, coaching, infrastructure development, and long‑term sustainability support. This article provides a full, detailed breakdown of the program, its purpose, structure, expectations, benefits, timeline, and application requirements. It is written to help organizations fully understand the opportunity and prepare a strong application. Program Purpose The Fellows Program expands on the existing Coffee Quality initiative by offering nonprofits a deeper, more sustained opportunity to build and strengthen their internal data and evaluation infrastructure. As stated in the document: “The Fellows Program is designed as a community of practice focused on supporting and strengthening the existing conditions for data and evaluation practices (infrastructure) of nonprofit organizations.” The program aims to ensure organizations can: Use data and evaluation findings to inform decisions and improve programmatic impact. Promote sustainable data practices that preserve institutional knowledge and support business continuity even during staff transitions. Program Structure Cohort Size Participation 12–14 organizations will be selected. Each organization must designate two staff members to participate. Participants must attend all sessions; missing more than one session disqualifies the organization from the current cycle. Duration Format The program includes: Initial 3‑month program Eight in‑person sessions (2 hours each) Coaching Article reviews Technical guidance Site visits One‑on‑one check‑ins Quarterly gatherings after the initial program Final site visits in 2027 A showcase ceremony with United Way staff, agency leadership, funders, and supporters Program Objectives Organizational Objectives Organizations will strengthen their internal systems and processes to measure impact through sustained data and evaluation practices. Individual Objectives Participants will build their personal skills in data literacy, evaluation, and CQI, improving both knowledge and application. Topics Covered in the Fellows Program The curriculum is comprehensive and designed to build a strong foundation for sustainable data practices. Topics include: Assessing organizational data and evaluation maturity Data literacy CQI culture Equitable data infrastructures Inventory and quality improvement Ensuring sustainability Meaningful use of data Enhancement of existing practices Strengthening soft and hard infrastructures Foundational elements for sustainable data collection Clear strategies for collecting and using data Logic model development Evaluation and CQI planning Documenting and strengthening data practices Data dictionaries (codebooks) Analysis procedure documentation Output, outcome, and target measurement Streamlining and demystifying reporting Reducing reporting burden Improving efficiency Using data for learning and improvement Distinguishing analysis vs. interpretation Using visualizations to communicate value Sustainability strategies Embedding long‑term data and evaluation practices Participation Requirements Time Commitment Participants must commit to: Attending all eight in‑person sessions Participating in site visits Engaging in coaching and technical guidance Attending the showcase ceremony The document states: “If more than one session is missed by both participants, the organization will be invited to join the next cycle of the Fellows Program.” Initial Program Schedule (Deadline Included) All sessions occur at United Way of Greater Houston (50 Waugh Drive, Houston, TX 77007) from 9 AM – 11 AM . August 2026 Friday, August 14 Friday, August 28 September 2026 Friday, September 11 Friday, September 18 Friday, September 25 October 2026 Friday, October 2 Friday, October 9 Thursday, October 22 (Showcase) Site visits occur during September October 2026. Capacity Building Grant (Up to $10,000) Each selected organization receives a stipend to support sustainable data and evaluation capacity. This can be used for: Soft Infrastructure Workshops, conferences, specialized courses Team learning activities Internal data discussions Coaching Research and development Hard Infrastructure Software licenses Data platforms, dashboards, CRMs Technical support or system developers Other Uses Organizations may propose additional uses that support data sustainability. Application Requirements Deadline Thursday, July 16, 2026 at 6 PM CST Required Submission Online Interest Form Letter of Interest (LOI) from CEO/Executive Director (max 2 pages) Optional Submission Letter of Support from Board Chair LOI Must Address: What the organization hopes to gain Three areas of improvement related to data and evaluation Key questions the organization wants to answer with data Leadership buy‑in and experience using data Names of the two staff participants Eligibility Nonprofits must: Be a 501(c)(3) Be located in Greater Houston area (Brazoria, Fort Bend, Harris, Montgomery, Waller counties) Interest Form Preview The interest form includes: Organizational information Budget range Participant details Likert‑scale questions on data practices Confidence ratings Organizational ability ratings Apply here For more information about this opportunity visit here Discover more fellowship opportunities here The post Coffee Quality 2026 Fellows Program: A Comprehensive Guide for Nonprofits Strengthening Data Evaluation Capacity appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Richard Rooney Postdoctoral Fellowship in African Studies at the University of Toronto
summary: Richard Rooney Postdoctoral Fellowship in African Studies at the University of Toronto The Richard Rooney Postdoctoral Fellowship in African Studies is a prestigious, fully funded academic opportunity offered by the African Studies Centre at the University of Toronto , housed within the Faculty of Arts and Science. With a long-standing legacy dating back to 1978, African Studies at the University of Toronto has grown into one of the most respected undergraduate programs in Canada and a leading hub for African-focused scholarship in North America. This fellowship represents a significant investment in emerging scholars whose work advances innovative, interdisciplinary, and socially engaged research on Africa and its global diasporas. Supported by a generous endowment from Mr. Richard Rooney, the fellowship aims to nurture new voices in African Studies while strengthening the Centre’s commitment to public scholarship, social justice, inclusivity, and community engagement. About the African Studies Centre The African Studies Centre serves as a vibrant intellectual, cultural, and social space for students, researchers, and community partners. It is deeply committed to: Epistemic diversity Decolonizing approaches to knowledge Interdisciplinary and transdisciplinary research Community engagement and public scholarship The Centre brings together scholars from the humanities, social sciences, creative arts, and other fields to explore the complexities of African societies and their diasporas. Fellowship Overview The Richard Rooney Postdoctoral Fellowship offers an exceptional opportunity for early-career scholars to develop and expand their research within a supportive academic environment. Fellows will engage with faculty mentors, participate in Centre activities, and contribute to knowledge dissemination locally, across Africa, and globally. Area of Research The fellowship welcomes proposals across a broad spectrum of African Studies, including: Social sciences Humanities Creative arts Interdisciplinary and transdisciplinary fields Research grounded in Africa and its diasporas Projects advancing decolonizing methodologies Key Responsibilities of the Fellow The selected fellow will undertake a range of scholarly and community-oriented activities, including: Developing and executing an independent research program that contributes meaningfully to African Studies. Collaborating with faculty mentors and engaging with interdisciplinary colleagues. Participating actively in the Centre’s intellectual life , including seminars, workshops, events, and public scholarship initiatives. Contributing to knowledge dissemination within Canada, across Africa, and internationally. Supporting the Centre’s mission of social justice, inclusivity, and community engagement. Salary and Benefits Annual Salary: $70,000 Benefits: Included Term: 12 months Hours: Full-time (40 hours per week) Note: If the collective agreement minimum rate is higher, the collective agreement rate will apply. Eligibility Requirements Applicants must meet the following criteria: Completion of a PhD between June 30, 2022 and June 30, 2026 Demonstrated academic excellence and strong research potential Eligibility to begin the fellowship by November 1, 2026 Commitment to public intellectual work and community engagement (an asset) The fellowship is open to domestic and international candidates . Application Instructions Applicants must submit one single PDF file named LastName_FirstName.pdf containing: Letter of application (max. 3 pages) including: Cover letter Statement of research and contributions One-page statement describing how the applicant will contribute to the African Studies Centre and the University of Toronto Detailed CV Two-page research proposal outlining: Project description Importance and impact Alignment with academic/professional goals Intended contributions Names and contact information for three academic references Deadline: July 10, 2026 Expected Start Date: November 1, 2026 Only shortlisted candidates will be contacted. For more information about this opportunity and to apply visit here Discover more fellowship opportunities here The post Richard Rooney Postdoctoral Fellowship in African Studies at the University of Toronto appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: The 2026 GDPC Spotlight Research Grants: Advancing Principled and Accountable Use of Technology in Humanitarian Action
summary: The 2026 GDPC Spotlight Research Grants: Advancing Principled and Accountable Use of Technology in Humanitarian Action The Global Disaster Preparedness Center (GDPC), in partnership with the French Red Cross Foundation, has officially launched the 2026 Spotlight Research Grants Program , a major funding initiative designed to strengthen global understanding of how technology—especially emerging ICTs and artificial intelligence—can be used responsibly, ethically, and effectively in humanitarian contexts. According to the document, “This initiative aims to deepen our understanding of how ICTs including emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence (AI) can be applied humanely, ethically, and responsibly in humanitarian contexts…” This program is specifically tailored to support researchers from low‑ and middle‑income countries (LMICs) and encourages diverse, community‑centered perspectives on digital transformation in humanitarian work. Program Overview The 2026 GDPC Spotlight Research Grants Program provides USD $10,000 to university‑affiliated researchers conducting original studies on the responsible use of technology in humanitarian action. Research projects may last up to eight months , with a final submission deadline of June 30, 2027 . The program supports applied, impactful research that can inform: Policy development Humanitarian programming Community‑level action Ethical technology deployment Organizational planning Research may be conducted in English or French , and translation costs may be included in the project budget if needed. The application deadline is 31 July 2026 at 23:59 UTC . Background: Why This Research Matters The global digital landscape is rapidly evolving, yet access remains unequal. The document highlights that “2.6 billion people globally are without internet access” and only “27% of people use the internet in low income countries.” As humanitarian organizations increasingly rely on ICTs—such as satellite imagery, mobile money, digital mapping, and AI‑enabled tools—new opportunities and risks emerge: Opportunities Improved disaster impact mapping Enhanced decision‑making through data analytics Faster information sharing Technology‑enabled farming Automation of administrative tasks Expanded access to underserved communities Risks Digital divides and inequity Cybercrime and misinformation Mental health harms Biases embedded in technology Threats to neutrality, impartiality, and independence Exposure to actors not bound by humanitarian principles The document warns that “Technologies can entrench or exacerbate existing inequities… They can expose people and communities to new risks in online and digital spaces…” This grant program aims to address these challenges through rigorous, community‑based research. Research Topics Applicants must align their proposals with one of the following priority areas: Topic 1: Community Needs Digital Realities Needs‑Aligned ICTs – Understanding affected people’s concerns and priorities regarding digital tools. Community Adaptation of ICTs – How communities repurpose, adapt, or abandon technologies over time. Topic 2: ICT Solution Feasibility Implementation Operational Constraints – Infrastructure, skills, funding, governance challenges in LMIC humanitarian settings. Digital Maturity Context – How varying levels of digital readiness affect outcomes. Humanitarian Principle Alignment – How aligning technology with humanitarian principles influences results. Topic 3: Data Protection in Humanitarian Action Personal Data Handling – How disaster‑affected people perceive and respond to data collection. Data Privacy Accountability – Effective feedback loops and complaint systems. Community Trust – How incidents like data breaches or misinformation affect trust. Eligibility Requirements Applicants must: Be affiliated with an accredited university (PhD candidates, post‑docs, faculty). Be nationals of low‑ or middle‑income countries . Conduct research focused on LMIC contexts. Work within social science or humanities disciplines (e.g., sociology, anthropology, psychology, law, human geography). Submit original, plagiarism‑free research. Complete the project within 1 November 2026 – 30 June 2027 . Selection Criteria Proposals will be evaluated based on: Scientific merit and rigor Relevance to defined research topics Impact potential for humanitarian policy and practice Ethical considerations and mitigation strategies Qualifications of the research team Realistic and justified budget Clear timeline and feasibility Cross‑collaboration and partnerships The document states that proposals should “Use community‑based or participatory methodologies” and “Critically assess technology, not assume its benefit.” Application Package Requirements Applicants must submit: Applicant Information Project Summary Project Description Budget Proposal (USD) Research Team Composition + CVs Letters of Collaboration Eligibility Form Program Timeline Application Deadline: 31 July 2026 (23:59 UTC) Program Duration: 1 November 2026 – 30 June 2027 Final Paper Deadline: 30 June 2027 Applicants are strongly encouraged to submit early to avoid technical issues. Apply here For more information about this opportunity visit here Discover more grant opportunities here The post The 2026 GDPC Spotlight Research Grants: Advancing Principled and Accountable Use of Technology in Humanitarian Action appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Future Leaders in the World of Work 2026: Complete Guide to the ITCILO South-South University Programme in Italy and Switzerland for Youth Leaders, Researchers, and Young Professionals
summary: The Future Leaders in the World of Work – South-South University 2026 is now accepting applications from ambitious university students, aspiring researchers, innovators, youth representatives, and early-career professionals who want to build careers in international development, multilateral diplomacy, sustainable development, and South-South and Triangular Cooperation (SSTC). Organized by the International Training Centre of the International Labour Organization (ITCILO) in cooperation with its knowledge partners and delivered within the framework of the Turin School of Development , this flagship programme equips participants with the knowledge, skills, networks, and career guidance needed to become future leaders supporting human-centred development across the Global South. Taking place from 27 July to 7 August 2026 , the programme combines face-to-face learning in Turin, Italy , a study visit to Geneva, Switzerland , practical workshops, career coaching, exposure to United Nations agencies, and opportunities to develop actionable proposals addressing the future of work. This comprehensive guide explains everything you need to know about the programme, including its objectives, benefits, eligibility requirements, learning structure, application process, costs, important dates, and why it represents a valuable opportunity for future global leaders. What Is the Future Leaders in the World of Work Programme? Future Leaders in the World of Work is a flagship international training programme under the South-South University , designed to prepare the next generation of professionals who are committed to advancing sustainable development through South-South and Triangular Cooperation. The programme recognizes that the future of employment, international cooperation, and sustainable development is being reshaped by major global transformations, including: Emerging technologies Climate change Demographic transitions Trade and investment shifts Digital transformation Changing labour markets New forms of multilateral cooperation Participants explore how these global trends influence employment, development policy, and international cooperation while designing innovative responses that promote decent work and sustainable development. The programme also supports young professionals interested in careers with: United Nations agencies International organizations Development institutions Government ministries International NGOs Multilateral development organizations Global policy institutions About the South-South University The South-South University is implemented through collaboration between ITCILO and its knowledge partners within the Turin School of Development. Its primary mission is to support ambitious young leaders who wish to contribute to: Human-centred development South-South Cooperation Triangular Cooperation International development Social justice Decent work Sustainable economic transformation Rather than offering only classroom-based instruction, the programme emphasizes practical engagement, collaboration, and policy-oriented problem solving. Why This Programme Matters The world of work is evolving rapidly. Artificial intelligence, automation, environmental challenges, digital transformation, and shifting geopolitical dynamics are changing how societies develop and how labour markets function. Countries in the Global South increasingly rely on South-South and Triangular Cooperation to: Share successful development models Exchange technical expertise Promote sustainable economic growth Build resilience Strengthen social inclusion The Future Leaders in the World of Work programme prepares participants to contribute meaningfully to these efforts while positioning themselves for future leadership roles in international organizations. Programme Dates The 2026 edition will take place from: 27 July – 7 August 2026 The programme consists of activities in both Italy and Switzerland. Italy Turin 27–29 July 2026 1–7 August 2026 Switzerland Geneva 30–31 July 2026 The programme is delivered entirely in English. Programme Structure The programme combines several complementary learning experiences. 1. Study Visit to Geneva Participants travel to Geneva to gain first-hand exposure to international organizations. Highlights include: Guest lectures at ILO Headquarters Learning about multilateral diplomacy Understanding UN operations Exploring international careers Visiting selected Geneva-based organizations Participants also meet representatives from Human Resources departments who explain career pathways within the United Nations system. 2. Thematic Workshops Interactive workshops focus on: Human-centred development South-South Cooperation Sustainable development Future of work Global labour challenges Participants work collaboratively to develop action-oriented proposals addressing global development priorities. 3. Personalized Career Coaching One of the programme s distinguishing features is individualized coaching. Participants receive support from international career coaches who help them: Clarify career goals Develop long-term career plans Build international career strategies Prepare for careers within the UN system Identify opportunities in multilateral organizations Each participant develops a realistic three-to-five-year professional roadmap. What Participants Will Learn Throughout the programme, participants examine major issues influencing international development. Topics include: Future of work Sustainable development Human-centred technology Climate change Trade and investment Demographic transitions International development Multilateral diplomacy South-South Cooperation Triangular Cooperation Social justice Decent work Sustainable labour markets The curriculum encourages participants to formulate innovative solutions capable of supporting equitable and sustainable development. Career Development Opportunities A major strength of the programme is its emphasis on career preparation. Participants receive guidance that includes: UN career pathways Career planning Professional coaching Networking strategies Leadership development International career preparation According to ITCILO, participants benefit from career coaching typically reserved for UN fellows. Who Can Apply? The programme welcomes applicants from around the world who are passionate about international development and the future of work. Eligible applicants include: Youth representatives University students Innovators Aspiring researchers Young professionals Professionals should generally be within five years of graduation at the time of application. Researchers should be enrolled in: Full-time taught degree programmes Full-time research degree programmes Academic Backgrounds Applicants may come from numerous disciplines relevant to the International Labour Organization, including: Law Economics Environmental Science Government Political Science International Relations Population Studies Public Policy Development Studies Applicants from related disciplines are also encouraged to apply. Eligibility Requirements Applicants should demonstrate: Commitment to South-South and Triangular Cooperation Interest in sustainable development Passion for the future of work Strong motivation to support international development Interest in the work of the International Labour Organization Fluency in English Knowledge of French or Spanish is considered an asset but is not mandatory. Motivation Interviews Shortlisted applicants will be invited to participate in motivation interviews. During these interviews, candidates should explain: Their academic journey Professional experience Motivation for joining the programme Career aspirations How the programme aligns with their future goals Applicants are encouraged to clearly connect their background with the objectives of the programme. Open House Webinars Prospective applicants can participate in Open House Webinars designed to help them prepare competitive applications. Topics include: Application guidance Motivation interview preparation Understanding programme objectives Exploring South-South Cooperation Learning about future work trends These webinars also provide opportunities to interact directly with programme coordinators. Campus Experience Participants study at the ITCILO Campus located along the banks of the Po River in Turin. The campus offers: Safe learning environment International atmosphere Modern facilities Opportunities for networking Access to the United Nations Campus Turin has hosted the United Nations Campus since 1964 and is home to: International Training Centre of the ILO United Nations System Staff College UN Interregional Crime and Justice Research Institute Study Visit to Geneva Participants spend two days in Geneva exploring: International Labour Organization Other UN agencies International organizations NGOs Activities include: Expert lectures Institutional visits Career sessions Human Resources discussions Multilateral diplomacy exposure Optional Cultural Excursions Participants may also register for optional day trips to: Venice Nice Monaco These cultural activities complement the learning experience while allowing participants to explore Europe. Certificate Participants who successfully complete all programme requirements receive an official ITCILO Certificate of Participation . The certificate is issued in Digital Credentials Format , allowing recipients to: Share on LinkedIn Include on CVs Showcase professional development Demonstrate international learning experience Programme Costs Programme fees are: Tuition: €2,345 Subsistence: €2,005 Total Cost: €4,350 Applicants should review programme details for payment arrangements and additional travel considerations. Important Dates Applicants should carefully note the following timeline: Programme Dates: 27 July–7 August 2026 Application Deadline: 5 July 2026 Language: English Delivery Mode: Face-to-face The application deadline has been extended to 5 July 2026 for participants who already possess, or do not require, an entry visa for Italy. Why You Should Apply This programme offers much more than classroom learning. Participants benefit from: International networking Exposure to the United Nations system Professional coaching Leadership development Practical workshops Career guidance Policy engagement Cross-cultural collaboration International experience Official ITCILO certification It is particularly valuable for young professionals seeking careers in: International organizations Sustainable development Public policy Labour and employment Global governance International cooperation Diplomacy Click HERE to learn more about the Future Leaders in the World of Work 2026. How to Apply Interested applicants should submit their application through the Official ITCILO Application Portal before the application deadline. Applicants are encouraged to: Review all eligibility requirements. Prepare a strong application. Demonstrate motivation for international development. Highlight academic and professional achievements. Show commitment to South-South and Triangular Cooperation. Prepare for the motivation interview if shortlisted. Download the Brochure HERE Conclusion The Future Leaders in the World of Work – South-South University 2026 represents an exceptional opportunity for ambitious students, researchers, innovators, and young professionals seeking to build careers in international development, sustainable development, and multilateral cooperation. Through an immersive learning experience spanning Turin and Geneva, participants gain direct exposure to the work of the International Labour Organization, United Nations agencies, and leading experts working on the future of work. The combination of practical workshops, policy discussions, personalized career coaching, networking opportunities, and international study visits equips participants with the knowledge, confidence, and global perspective needed to contribute meaningfully to sustainable development and South-South Cooperation initiatives. With an application deadline of 5 July 2026 , interested candidates are encouraged to submit their applications as early as possible, prepare thoroughly for the motivation interview, and take advantage of this unique opportunity to become part of a global community of future leaders committed to advancing decent work, social justice, and human-centred development. Discover more global Fellowship and Leadership Development opportunities on OFY. The post Future Leaders in the World of Work 2026: Complete Guide to the ITCILO South-South University Programme in Italy and Switzerland for Youth Leaders, Researchers, and Young Professionals appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Deloitte Graduate Program 2026 Applications Open: Launch Your Career with One of the World’s Leading Professional Services Firms
summary: Every successful career starts with the right opportunity, and Deloitte is offering aspiring professionals exactly that. Applications are now open for the 2026 Deloitte Graduate and Vacationer Programs , providing university students and recent graduates with the chance to develop future-ready skills, gain hands-on experience, and begin a rewarding professional journey with one of the world s leading consulting and professional services organizations. Whether you aspire to work in consulting, technology, audit, tax, strategy, engineering, or other professional disciplines, Deloitte s Graduate Program is designed to help talented individuals build meaningful careers while making a real impact from their very first day. Applications are open from 1 July 2026 until 22 July 2026 , so interested candidates are encouraged to apply before the deadline. About the Deloitte Graduate Program The Deloitte Graduate Program is designed for students who are completing their university studies during mid-to-late 2026 or graduates who have completed their degrees within the previous two years. Successful applicants will begin their careers with Deloitte in 2027. From the moment graduates join Deloitte, they become part of a diverse cohort of professionals representing various academic disciplines, backgrounds, and experiences. The program provides an inclusive environment where participants collaborate, learn together, and establish lasting professional relationships. Unlike many graduate programs that limit applicants based on their academic background, Deloitte recognizes that talent comes in many forms. Graduates are encouraged to explore different career pathways regardless of their degree specialization. For example, Deloitte welcomes: Technology graduates working in Tax Engineers contributing to Audit IT graduates joining Strategy Professionals from various disciplines across multiple business units Your university degree serves as a starting point rather than a limitation. Why Choose Deloitte? Deloitte believes that exceptional careers are built in environments where employees are trusted, supported, and inspired to perform at their best. Successful graduates will enjoy numerous benefits throughout their professional journey. 1. Make an Impact from Day One Rather than spending months observing, graduates immediately contribute to meaningful projects that create value for clients, businesses, and communities. Participants gain practical experience while working alongside experienced professionals on real business challenges. 2. Build Future-Ready Skills The program equips graduates with practical and professional skills required to succeed in today s rapidly changing business environment. Participants strengthen competencies such as: Problem-solving Communication Leadership Collaboration Innovation Critical thinking Client engagement Professional consulting skills 3. Graduate Learning Academy One of the highlights of the program is Deloitte s Graduate Learning Academy. This structured learning experience includes: Interactive workshops Creative challenges Practical learning activities Professional coaching Skill development sessions Confidence-building exercises Networking opportunities The academy helps graduates establish strong foundations for long-term career success. 4. Dedicated Career Coaching Every graduate receives personalized career support through: A dedicated career coach Professional mentoring Peer support networks Continuous feedback Career guidance Personal development planning Graduates are never expected to navigate their professional journey alone. 5. Continuous Learning Opportunities Learning continues beyond onboarding. Participants have access to: E-learning platforms Professional development resources Ongoing mentoring Skills enhancement programs Career progression opportunities This commitment to continuous learning enables graduates to adapt and thrive throughout their careers. 6. Explore Diverse Career Paths Deloitte offers opportunities across numerous industries and business areas, allowing graduates to discover where their strengths and interests align. Participants can explore multiple professional pathways while building deep technical and industry expertise. Who Can Apply? The Graduate Program is intended for applicants who meet one of the following criteria: Students completing university studies between mid-2026 and the end of 2026. Graduates who completed their degree within the last two years. Individuals ready to begin a graduate career with Deloitte in 2027. Applicants from a wide variety of academic disciplines are encouraged to apply. What Makes Deloitte Different? Deloitte values diversity of thought, education, and experience. Rather than restricting career paths based on university degrees, Deloitte encourages graduates to pursue opportunities that match their interests, skills, and potential. This flexible approach allows graduates to develop careers that align with both their professional aspirations and evolving business needs. Skills Matcher Quiz Applicants who are unsure which Deloitte team best suits their interests can complete Deloitte s Skills Matcher Quiz. The quiz helps candidates identify career opportunities that align with their: Skills Interests Preferences Career aspirations This makes it easier to choose the most suitable graduate pathway before submitting an application. Application Deadline Applications open: 1 July 2026 Application deadline: 22 July 2026 Interested applicants should complete and submit their applications before the closing date. How to Apply Prospective applicants should: Explore Deloitte s available Graduate and Vacationer opportunities. Learn about the different business areas and roles. Complete the Skills Matcher Quiz if needed. Submit an online application before 22 July 2026 . Click here for more and to apply Starting your career with Deloitte offers far more than a graduate job—it provides an opportunity to learn, grow, build meaningful professional relationships, and contribute to impactful work while developing a successful long-term career. Click here for more global internship opportunities The post Deloitte Graduate Program 2026 Applications Open: Launch Your Career with One of the World s Leading Professional Services Firms appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Indo-Pacific NGO Blended Finance Accelerator 2026: Receive Up to AUD 840,000 (Approximately USD 550,000) Plus Expert Acceleration Support to Scale Climate and Gender Equality Solutions Across the Indo-Pacific
summary: Organizations working to solve climate change and gender inequality often face one common challenge—accessing sufficient financing to scale their impact. While grants remain important, attracting private investment has become increasingly critical for achieving sustainable development goals at scale. To address this challenge, the Australian Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT) , in partnership with Convergence Blended Finance , has launched the Indo-Pacific NGO Blended Finance Accelerator , a five-year initiative worth AUD 10 million . The programme is designed to help NGOs develop innovative blended finance solutions that unlock private investment for climate action and gender equality initiatives throughout the Indo-Pacific region. The Accelerator combines catalytic grant funding with technical acceleration support, enabling NGOs to transform innovative ideas into investment-ready financial vehicles capable of delivering measurable environmental and social impact. Application Deadline: 20 July 2026 What is the Indo-Pacific NGO Blended Finance Accelerator? The Indo-Pacific NGO Blended Finance Accelerator is a comprehensive programme that helps NGOs bridge the gap between development funding and private capital. Although blended finance has expanded significantly across the Indo-Pacific, there remains a shortage of investable projects capable of attracting commercial investors. NGOs possess deep local knowledge, technical expertise, and trusted community relationships, making them well positioned to design impactful financial solutions. However, they currently sponsor only a small percentage of blended finance transactions globally due to several institutional and financial barriers. The Accelerator seeks to change this by providing financial support, expert guidance, investor connections, and technical resources that enable NGOs to create scalable blended finance vehicles capable of attracting long-term investment. Total Programme Funding The programme is backed by a five-year AUD 10 million investment and provides both financial support and capacity-building services. Available catalytic grants include: Up to AUD 300,000 (approximately USD 200,000 ) for early-stage blended finance initiatives. Up to AUD 840,000 (approximately USD 550,000 ) for advanced-stage and scaling initiatives. All grants are disbursed in Australian Dollars (AUD). What is Blended Finance? Blended finance refers to the strategic use of public, philanthropic, or concessional funding to attract private investment into projects that generate both financial returns and positive development outcomes. Rather than relying solely on donor funding, blended finance combines different sources of capital to create sustainable financing mechanisms capable of delivering large-scale impact. Examples include: Investment funds Project finance vehicles Private companies Bonds Financing facilities Results-based financing mechanisms Other innovative financial instruments Programme Objectives The Accelerator aims to: Increase private investment for climate action. Strengthen financing for gender equality initiatives. Build a pipeline of investment-ready blended finance projects. Support NGOs in becoming investment managers and impact managers. Expand partnerships between NGOs and private investors. Deliver measurable environmental and social outcomes. Strengthen financial sustainability for development initiatives. Priority Impact Areas Applications should focus on one or both of the programme s major themes. Climate Action Projects may support: Climate change mitigation Climate adaptation Clean energy Sustainable cities Responsible consumption Life below water Life on land These areas align with Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): SDG 7 – Affordable and Clean Energy SDG 11 – Sustainable Cities and Communities SDG 12 – Responsible Consumption and Production SDG 13 – Climate Action SDG 14 – Life Below Water SDG 15 – Life on Land Gender Equality Projects should demonstrate a strong commitment to gender equality through: Women s empowerment Financial inclusion Healthcare Education Gender-responsive investment models Gender integration throughout project design, implementation, and governance Applications should align with SDG 5 – Gender Equality . What Support Will Selected NGOs Receive? Successful applicants receive more than funding. The programme provides: 1. Catalytic Grant Funding Funding supports activities such as: Feasibility studies Legal structuring Financial modelling Vehicle design Investment pipeline development Fundraising Pilot implementation Governance development Impact measurement systems 2. Acceleration Support Participants also gain access to: Professional training Curated market intelligence Data and analytics Technical mentoring Peer-learning opportunities Investor introductions Ecosystem partnerships Ongoing feedback from blended finance experts Guidance on engaging commercial investors Types of Grants Available Stage 1: Scoping and Feasibility Maximum funding: Up to AUD 300,000 Suitable for NGOs that are exploring blended finance opportunities. Funding may support: Financial model development Vehicle design Legal structuring Market assessments Stakeholder consultations Investment pipeline development Governance framework design Initial impact assessment Applicants should already have: An initial theory of change Early scoping work Potential partners identified Stage 2: Proof of Concept and Pilot Maximum funding: Up to AUD 840,000 Funding supports: Investor outreach Legal refinement Governance improvements Impact framework development Pilot investments Investment pipeline expansion Applicants should demonstrate: Completed feasibility work Initial financial model Internal and external support Key implementation partners At least one soft or hard investor commitment Stage 3: Scale-Up and Expansion Maximum funding: Up to AUD 840,000 Funding may be used for: Vehicle expansion Operational scaling Additional fundraising Legal improvements Pipeline growth General Partner (GP) capital commitments Preparing vehicles for larger investment rounds Applicants should already have: An investment-ready or operational vehicle Anchor investors Committed implementation partners Defined NGO roles Eligible Financial Vehicles Projects may include: Investment funds Bonds Project finance structures Private companies Financing facilities Results-based financing mechanisms Other innovative blended finance instruments Countries Eligible for Project Implementation Projects must focus on one or more eligible Indo-Pacific countries, including: Bangladesh Bhutan Cambodia Indonesia Laos Mongolia Myanmar Nepal Pakistan Philippines Sri Lanka Timor-Leste Vietnam Eligible Pacific Island nations include: Cook Islands Federated States of Micronesia Fiji Kiribati Nauru Niue Papua New Guinea Republic of Palau Republic of the Marshall Islands Samoa Solomon Islands Tokelau Tonga Tuvalu Vanuatu Applicants may be based anywhere in the world, provided their proposed blended finance vehicle serves eligible Indo-Pacific countries. Eligibility Requirements Applicants must: Be an NGO applying independently or with consortium partners. Demonstrate a clear need for grant funding. Develop a blended finance solution capable of attracting private investment. Hold a significant implementation role. Obtain internal leadership approval. Target eligible Indo-Pacific countries. Focus on climate action, gender equality, or both. Present a credible pathway toward investment readiness and financial sustainability. Evaluation Criteria Applications will be assessed based on: Development Impact Projects should generate measurable environmental and social benefits, particularly for underserved populations including: Women Indigenous communities Persons with disabilities Communities in Least Developed Countries (LDCs) Frontier markets Scalability and Replicability Proposals should demonstrate potential to: Mobilize significant private investment Expand across multiple markets Serve as models for future blended finance initiatives Additionality Applicants should explain: Why blended finance is necessary How the project addresses financing gaps Why grant funding is essential Gender Integration Strong proposals integrate gender considerations throughout project design rather than treating gender as an additional feature. Investment Readiness Projects should demonstrate: Strong governance Financial sustainability Investor engagement Commercial viability Risk management Internal Organizational Commitment Applicants should show: Leadership support Dedicated implementation teams Alignment with organizational strategy Stakeholder Partnerships Preference is given to proposals with: Financial partners Implementation partners Regional stakeholder support Track Record Applicants should demonstrate: Relevant technical expertise Local implementation experience Strong partnerships Capacity to manage blended finance initiatives Why This Programme Matters Traditional grant funding alone cannot meet the growing financing needs for climate resilience and gender equality across the Indo-Pacific. By combining grants with technical expertise and private investment, the Indo-Pacific NGO Blended Finance Accelerator enables NGOs to create sustainable financing solutions that continue generating impact long after grant funding has ended. The programme also strengthens NGO capacity to engage commercial investors, manage innovative financial vehicles, and build scalable development solutions capable of transforming communities across the region. Key Dates Application Deadline: 20 July 2026 Interested NGOs are encouraged to prepare their concept notes well before the deadline to maximize their chances of success. title: GreenLight Fund Expression of Interest 2026: Receive Up to US$600,000 in Unrestricted Funding to Expand Your Nonprofit Across the United States
summary: Nonprofit organizations with proven, evidence-based programs often reach a stage where their impact has been demonstrated, but expanding into new communities requires significant financial investment, strategic planning, staffing support, and strong local partnerships. GreenLight Fund is addressing this challenge by inviting eligible U.S.-based nonprofit organizations to submit an Expression of Interest (EOI) for future funding and expansion opportunities. Through this initiative, selected organizations may receive up to US$600,000 in unrestricted funding over four years, together with comprehensive strategic support designed to help successful nonprofit models launch and thrive in new communities throughout the United States. Unlike many traditional grant opportunities that provide funding alone, GreenLight Fund combines long-term financial investment with practical implementation support through its nationally recognized Beyond the Check partnership model. Expression of Interest Deadline Expression of Interest Pilot Deadline: July 17, 2026 Organizations interested in joining GreenLight Fund s future partnership pipeline should submit their Expression of Interest before the deadline. It is important to note that submitting an Expression of Interest is not the same as submitting a full grant application. Rather, it allows GreenLight Fund to learn more about your organization and determine whether it aligns with current or future partnership opportunities. Organizations whose missions and programs align with GreenLight Fund s model may later be invited into additional review and selection stages. About GreenLight Fund GreenLight Fund is a national nonprofit organization that works alongside local communities across the United States to improve opportunities for inclusive prosperity. Rather than imposing predetermined solutions, GreenLight partners directly with communities to identify their most pressing unmet needs before selecting proven nonprofit organizations capable of addressing those challenges. The organization focuses on removing barriers that prevent individuals and families from achieving long-term economic mobility, particularly those rooted in racial and economic inequities. Today, GreenLight operates across multiple sites throughout the United States and continues expanding its national presence. Each year, every GreenLight site follows a structured community-driven process to identify one critical local challenge and invest in a nonprofit organization capable of delivering measurable and sustainable results. Funding Benefits Organizations selected into GreenLight Fund s portfolio receive far more than financial support. Successful nonprofits may receive: Up to US$600,000 in unrestricted funding over four years. Multi-year investment designed to support long-term growth. Strategic guidance throughout expansion into new communities. Access to GreenLight Fund s Beyond the Check support model. Coaching on organizational strategy. Leadership and staffing support. Assistance overcoming implementation challenges. Partnership development with local organizations. Connections to additional funding opportunities. Access to GreenLight s national network of community leaders. Support from experienced local advisory boards. Ongoing relationship-building with stakeholders. Assistance developing sustainable local operations. Long-term accountability and performance measurement. Because the funding is unrestricted, organizations have greater flexibility to invest resources where they are most needed to achieve successful expansion. What Makes GreenLight Fund Different? Many grant programs simply award funding and expect organizations to implement projects independently. GreenLight Fund takes a different approach. Through its Beyond the Check model, GreenLight works as a long-term strategic partner throughout the expansion journey. This includes: Strategic coaching Local relationship building Staffing guidance Partnership development Fundraising support Leadership mentoring Sustainability planning Performance measurement The objective is not only to help nonprofits launch in a new city but also to ensure they become sustainable, community-supported organizations capable of creating lasting impact. Priority Impact Areas Although each GreenLight community identifies its own local priorities annually, the organization commonly supports nonprofit solutions addressing: Housing stability Education Youth opportunity Workforce development Economic mobility Health and well-being Family support Community resilience Reducing racial disparities Reducing economic disparities Inclusive prosperity initiatives Organizations operating in one or more of these focus areas are encouraged to explore the opportunity. Who Should Apply? GreenLight Fund is seeking nonprofit organizations that are prepared to scale successful programs into additional communities. Strong candidates typically: Operate as U.S.-based nonprofit organizations. Have a proven, evidence-based model. Demonstrate measurable outcomes. Can clearly show the impact of their work. Are interested in expanding into new U.S. communities. Address challenges related to economic mobility and inclusive prosperity. Have leadership committed to long-term growth and partnership. Organizations still testing early-stage ideas or pilot programs may not yet be ready for this opportunity, as GreenLight is specifically looking for solutions with demonstrated success that can be replicated effectively in new locations. Understanding the GreenLight Method GreenLight Fund follows a structured five-step process to identify and scale nonprofit solutions. 1. Discover GreenLight works closely with local communities, advisory committees, residents, and stakeholders to identify unmet community needs using research, local data, and extensive listening sessions. 2. Scout Once priority challenges have been identified, GreenLight searches nationally for nonprofit organizations with proven models capable of addressing those needs. 3. Select Potential organizations undergo careful evaluation before one nonprofit is selected as the strongest local fit. 4. Invest Selected organizations receive unrestricted funding together with strategic support to establish and expand operations within the new community. 5. Measure GreenLight works with partner organizations to establish measurable goals and track both programmatic and financial performance throughout the partnership. This structured approach helps ensure that investments produce measurable outcomes and long-term community impact. The GreenLight Partnership Journey Organizations interested in partnering with GreenLight Fund can expect several stages throughout the selection process. Stage 1: Interest and Exploration Organizations submit an Expression of Interest indicating their desire to be considered for future partnership opportunities. Stage 2: Pipeline Qualification GreenLight reviews submitted information to determine whether the organization aligns with its partnership model. Qualified organizations may be invited to an introductory conversation. Stage 3: Diligence and Alignment Organizations may be asked to provide additional documentation and participate in deeper discussions regarding program effectiveness, expansion readiness, and organizational capacity. Stage 4: Community-Driven Selection Finalists may present their work before a Selection Advisory Committee and engage directly with local community stakeholders. Stage 5: Partnership and Investment Each year, GreenLight selects one organization per site to expand into a new community. Selected organizations enter a formal partnership agreement outlining funding, expectations, strategic support, and shared accountability. Why This Opportunity Matters Scaling nonprofit programs successfully requires much more than financial investment. Organizations often need: Strong local partnerships. Community trust. Leadership coaching. Sustainable staffing structures. Fundraising guidance. Strategic planning. Long-term implementation support. GreenLight Fund addresses all of these needs simultaneously through its integrated funding and partnership approach. For nonprofits with proven models ready to reach more communities, this opportunity offers one of the most comprehensive expansion support programs currently available in the United States. Important Notes Expression of Interest Deadline: July 17, 2026 Submitting an Expression of Interest does not constitute a formal application. GreenLight Fund staff will review submissions to determine organizational alignment. Organizations that demonstrate strong alignment may be contacted for additional conversations or future selection cycles. Selection is based on community priorities, organizational readiness, measurable impact, and alignment with GreenLight Fund s mission. How to Apply Eligible nonprofit organizations should complete the GreenLight Fund Expression of Interest Form before the July 17, 2026 deadline. Click here for more and to apply Click here for more global grant opportunities The post GreenLight Fund Expression of Interest 2026: Receive Up to US$600,000 in Unrestricted Funding to Expand Your Nonprofit Across the United States appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Free SOUTHMOD Training Course 2026 on Tax and Benefit Microsimulation for Latin America | Hybrid Participation in Quito, Ecuador
summary: Professionals, researchers, policymakers, and academics interested in tax and social protection policy analysis are invited to register for the SOUTHMOD Training Course on Microsimulation for Tax and Benefit Analysis in Latin America , taking place 14–16 July 2026 in Quito, Ecuador . Organized by the United Nations University World Institute for Development Economics Research (UNU-WIDER) in collaboration with Facultad Latinoamericana de Ciencias Sociales (FLACSO Ecuador) , Universidad del Pacífico , the International Inequalities Institute at the London School of Economics (LSE) , and Pontificia Universidad Javeriana’s Observatorio Fiscal , this three-day hybrid training programme provides participants with practical skills in using tax-benefit microsimulation models to evaluate fiscal policies. The training is completely free of charge , open to all eligible participants, and will be delivered entirely in Spanish . Registration Status Registration is currently open. Training Dates 14 July 2026 – 16 July 2026 Venue Quito, Ecuador The course will be offered in a hybrid format , allowing participants to attend either in person or through virtual participation. About the SOUTHMOD Training Course The SOUTHMOD training course is designed to equip participants with practical knowledge and hands-on experience in using microsimulation models to analyze the impact of tax and social benefit policies across Latin America. Participants will learn how to evaluate how different fiscal policies affect: Household income Poverty levels Income inequality Government revenues Social protection outcomes Distribution of taxes and benefits The programme focuses on practical applications that support evidence-based policymaking and economic research. What is SOUTHMOD? SOUTHMOD is an internationally recognized research initiative that develops, updates, and maintains tax-benefit microsimulation models for low- and middle-income countries. These models combine: Household income data Household expenditure data National tax legislation Social benefit policies Using these datasets, researchers and policymakers can simulate proposed policy changes before implementation and estimate their likely economic and social impacts. This makes SOUTHMOD an essential tool for governments, researchers, and development organizations seeking to design more effective fiscal and social protection policies. Countries Covered by the Training This training focuses on four Latin American microsimulation models: ECUAMOD (Ecuador) COLMOD (Colombia) BOLMOD (Bolivia) PERUMOD (Peru) Participants will gain practical experience using these country-specific models to assess tax and benefit reforms. What Participants Will Learn Throughout the three-day programme, participants will receive practical instruction on how to: Use tax-benefit microsimulation software. Analyze household-level income and expenditure data. Evaluate fiscal policy reforms. Estimate poverty impacts. Measure inequality outcomes. Assess government revenue implications. Compare alternative tax and social benefit scenarios. Interpret simulation results for policymaking. The course emphasizes applied learning through real-world examples and hands-on exercises. Who Should Attend? The programme is intended for both experienced and first-time SOUTHMOD users. Ideal participants include: Government officials involved in fiscal policy. Public finance specialists. Social policy analysts. Academic researchers. University lecturers. Think tank professionals. Public institution analysts. Graduate students interested in tax and social policy. Anyone interested in evidence-based policymaking and fiscal analysis is encouraged to participate. Why Tax-Benefit Microsimulation Matters Tax-benefit microsimulation has become one of the most valuable tools for governments and researchers because it enables policymakers to test the likely consequences of policy reforms before implementation. These models help answer important policy questions, including: How will a proposed tax reform affect low-income households? Which social benefit programme is most effective at reducing poverty? What impact will policy changes have on government revenues? How will inequality change under different fiscal scenarios? Such evidence supports smarter, fairer, and more efficient public policy decisions. Global Reach of SOUTHMOD UNU-WIDER currently hosts fourteen tax-benefit microsimulation models covering countries across multiple regions. Africa Egypt Ethiopia Ghana Mozambique Rwanda Tanzania Uganda Zambia Latin America Bolivia Colombia Ecuador Peru Southeast Asia Viet Nam Other Zanzibar These models complement previously developed systems for South Africa and Namibia, making SOUTHMOD one of the world s leading tax-benefit microsimulation initiatives for developing economies. Organizing Institutions The training course is jointly organized by: United Nations University World Institute for Development Economics Research (UNU-WIDER) Facultad Latinoamericana de Ciencias Sociales (FLACSO Ecuador) Universidad del Pacífico International Inequalities Institute, London School of Economics (LSE) Pontificia Universidad Javeriana – Observatorio Fiscal This collaboration brings together leading experts in economics, public finance, social policy, and fiscal analysis. Benefits of Participating Participants will benefit from: Free professional training. Practical hands-on learning. Hybrid participation options. Exposure to internationally recognized policy tools. Improved analytical skills. Better understanding of tax-benefit modelling. Networking opportunities with researchers and policymakers. Knowledge applicable to fiscal policy design and evaluation. Enhanced research and policy analysis capabilities. Why You Should Register Whether you work in government, academia, public policy, or development research, this course offers an excellent opportunity to strengthen your expertise in tax-benefit microsimulation and evidence-based fiscal policymaking. By learning to use SOUTHMOD, participants will gain practical skills that can support better policy design, improve social protection systems, and contribute to reducing poverty and inequality across developing countries. Registration is free, and interested participants are encouraged to secure their place before the programme begins on 14 July 2026 . Apply Here For more information on this training opportunity visit here Discover more training opportunities on OFY here The post Free SOUTHMOD Training Course 2026 on Tax and Benefit Microsimulation for Latin America | Hybrid Participation in Quito, Ecuador appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: European Studies Review Job Fair 2026: Discover the Latest Internships, Traineeships, and Career Opportunities Across Europe
summary: Finding the right internship, traineeship, or entry-level job can be one of the most important steps toward building a successful international career. The European Studies Review (ESR) Job Fair brings together some of the latest opportunities offered by leading European institutions, international organizations, consulting firms, research institutes, and private-sector employers. Updated regularly and promoted every Wednesday on LinkedIn, the ESR Job Fair serves as a valuable resource for students, graduates, and young professionals seeking career opportunities in diplomacy, European Union affairs, economics, law, communications, finance, sustainability, research, and public policy. Whether you are looking to gain your first professional experience, develop specialized skills, or begin a long-term career within European institutions, this collection of opportunities provides an excellent starting point. Why Follow the European Studies Review Job Fair? The European Studies Review has become a trusted platform for promoting opportunities that help young professionals launch successful international careers. The Job Fair features vacancies from prestigious organizations across Europe, including European Union institutions, international organizations, consulting firms, financial institutions, research centers, and advocacy organizations. Applicants benefit from having multiple career opportunities available in one place, allowing them to explore positions that match their education, interests, and professional goals. Latest Opportunities Available 1. UNITAR – Multilateral Diplomacy Programme Traineeship Location: Geneva, Switzerland The United Nations Institute for Training and Research (UNITAR) is recruiting trainees for its Multilateral Diplomacy Programme. This opportunity provides participants with practical exposure to international diplomacy while supporting UNITAR s work in multilateral cooperation, global governance, and diplomatic capacity-building. Application Deadline: 8 July 2. European Central Bank – Financial Analysis Traineeship Location: Frankfurt am Main, Germany The European Central Bank (ECB) is offering a traineeship within the Directorate General Market Infrastructure and Payments. Successful candidates will gain valuable experience in financial analysis while working alongside experts responsible for Europe s financial infrastructure and payment systems. Application Deadline: 9 July 3. European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) – Multiple Traineeships Location: Paris, France ESMA is offering multiple traineeship positions for graduates interested in financial regulation, market supervision, and European financial governance. Participants will work alongside specialists responsible for maintaining the stability and integrity of European financial markets. Application Deadline: 15 July 4. European Economic and Social Committee – Traineeship Programme The European Economic and Social Committee offers traineeships that provide participants with practical experience in European policymaking while working within one of the European Union s advisory institutions. The programme allows trainees to understand how EU institutions operate and contribute to policy development. Application Deadline: 30 September 5. European Committee of the Regions – Traineeships The European Committee of the Regions offers traineeships for graduates interested in regional development, European governance, and local policymaking. Participants gain first-hand experience supporting one of the European Union s consultative bodies. Application Deadline: 30 September 6. British Chamber of Commerce (EU Belgium) – Committees Officer This position offers professionals an opportunity to support committee activities, stakeholder engagement, and organizational coordination within the British Chamber of Commerce. Application Deadline: 3 July 7. Avocats Sans Frontières – Research Assistant Intern Applicants interested in legal research, human rights, and international justice can apply for the Research Assistant Internship offered by Avocats Sans Frontières. The internship provides valuable practical experience supporting research activities within an international legal organization. Application Deadline: 12 July 8. Time Place Consulting – Advocacy Communication Intern Time Place Consulting is recruiting an Advocacy and Communication Intern. Successful applicants will gain hands-on experience supporting advocacy initiatives, strategic communication, stakeholder engagement, and policy communication. Application Deadline: 15 July 9. EIPA – EU Policy-Making Intern The European Institute of Public Administration (EIPA) offers an internship focused on European Union policy-making. Participants gain practical knowledge of EU governance, institutional processes, and policy implementation. Application Deadline: 15 July 10. EGMONT – Royal Institute for International Relations The Royal Institute for International Relations is offering internships within its Education and Training Programme. This opportunity is ideal for candidates interested in international affairs, diplomacy, security studies, and foreign policy. Application Deadline: 15 July 11. EUnited – Membership Communications Officer EUnited is recruiting a Membership and Communications Officer to support member engagement, communications strategies, and organizational activities. Application Deadline: 31 July 12. EY Belgium – Internship Payroll EY Belgium offers internship opportunities within its payroll team. Participants gain professional experience working within one of the world s leading professional services firms. Application Deadline: Rolling applications 13. Acumen Public Affairs – Trainee Consultant Opportunities Acumen Public Affairs is recruiting: Health Trainee Consultant Sustainability Trainee Consultant These opportunities allow graduates to gain consulting experience within health policy and sustainability sectors. Application Deadline: Rolling applications Benefits of Following the ESR Job Fair Applicants can benefit from: Access to multiple internship, traineeship, and job opportunities in one place. Opportunities with prestigious European institutions and international organizations. Career development in diplomacy, finance, communications, public policy, law, economics, sustainability, and international relations. Practical professional experience. Exposure to European policymaking and governance. Networking opportunities with leading professionals. A convenient weekly source of newly published career opportunities. Why You Should Apply Many of these organizations are internationally recognized and provide excellent environments for learning, professional development, and long-term career growth. Whether your ambition is to work within European Union institutions, international organizations, financial institutions, research institutes, or consulting firms, these opportunities can serve as important stepping stones toward achieving your career goals. Application Deadlines The opportunities featured in this Job Fair have different application deadlines: British Chamber of Commerce – 3 July UNITAR – 8 July European Central Bank – 9 July Avocats Sans Frontières – 12 July ESMA – 15 July Time Place Consulting – 15 July EIPA – 15 July EGMONT – 15 July EUnited – 31 July European Economic and Social Committee – 30 September European Committee of the Regions – 30 September EY Belgium – Rolling Deadline Acumen Public Affairs – Rolling Deadline Applicants are strongly encouraged to submit their applications well before the stated deadlines. How to Stay Updated European Studies Review publishes new career opportunities every Wednesday through its Job Fair. Follow the platform to stay informed about internships, traineeships, graduate programmes, fellowships, and job openings across Europe.
